title: Smartphone Ownership, Age of Smartphone Acquisition, and Health Outcomes in Early Adolescence.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data across baseline and multiple follow-up waves (2-year, 3-year, 4-year assessments) examining smartphone ownership and health outcomes.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data across three analyses at 2-year and 3-year follow-up assessments with N=10,588 (Analysis 1), N=6,739 (Analysis 2), and N=3,486 (Analysis 3).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Smartphone ownership (yes/no) and age of first smartphone acquisition measured as primary exposures; also measured problematic smartphone use via MPIQ.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Smartphone ownership and age of first smartphone acquisition measured as primary exposures; qualifying screen variables per criteria.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Reports associations between smartphone ownership/age of acquisition and health outcomes: depression diagnosis (KSADS-5), obesity (BMI), and insufficient sleep (Munich Chronotype Questionnaire). Logistic and linear mixed models with reported coefficients.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple inferential tests linking smartphone exposures to health outcomes (depression, obesity, insufficient sleep). Mixed models and logistic regressions reported throughout.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Multiple numeric results reported: e.g., smartphone ownership associated with depression, obesity, sleep; supplemental tables include ORs, CIs, p-values for binary outcomes and continuous measures.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric coefficients and p-values reported in supplemental tables (β, SE, p-values); example from supplemental methods: models with depression, BMI, and sleep duration outcomes.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original longitudinal analysis with mixed models testing associations of smartphone ownership/acquisition with health outcomes across three assessment timepoints.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis with three distinct analyses testing associations of smartphone ownership/acquisition with health outcomes.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Pediatrics (2026).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article (Pediatrics, 2026).

title: Developmental Pathways From Childhood Neurodevelopmental Traits to Early Adolescent Psychiatric Dimensions: The Role of Environmental and Lifestyle Factors.

FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Measured screen use (duration of screen use distinguishing between social media, social engagement, passive watching, active gaming) as lifestyle factor at ages 10 and 11.

FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Mediation models testing screen use as mediator of neurodevelopmental-to-psychiatric pathways. Sleep problems, school involvement, and other lifestyle factors mediated associations; direct and indirect effects reported.

title: Sociodemographic Associations With Early Smartphone Ownership in US Adolescents
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Paper explicitly uses ABCD Study data collected from 11,878 children and primary caregivers at baseline (mean age 9.9 years) with annual follow-up assessments through 2016-2018; analytic sample N=9,475.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes ABCD baseline data from 9,475 children aged 9.9 years enrolled 2016-2018 with annual follow-ups.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Smartphone ownership and age of first smartphone acquisition measured via parent report; smartphone ownership operationalized as early (<10 years) vs. later (≥10 years).
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Smartphone ownership age/timing measured via parent report: mean age 10.9 years, dichotomized as <10 years vs. ≥10 years.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Cross-sectional multivariable logistic regression examining associations between sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, marital status, parental education) and early smartphone ownership. ORs and 95% CIs reported.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multivariable logistic regression testing associations between smartphone ownership (exposure) and sociodemographic predictors (sex, race/ethnicity, household income, parental marital status, parental education—all non-screen variables).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric results include ORs and 95% CIs for sociodemographic associations with early smartphone ownership (e.g., Black OR=3.01 [2.50–3.64], p<.001; Female OR=1.38 [1.19–1.60], p<.001).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Odds ratios reported with 95% CIs: Black youth OR=3.01 (95% CI 2.50–3.64), girls OR=1.41 (95% CI 1.24–1.60), single parent OR=2.09 (95% CI 1.77–2.46), <bachelor's degree OR=1.72 (95% CI 1.46–2.02).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original cross-sectional empirical analysis of ABCD data examining sociodemographic predictors of early smartphone ownership.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using multivariable logistic regression with propensity weights and cluster-robust SEs.

title: Higher recreational screen time and lower step count are associated with higher cardiovascular disease risk in early adolescence
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data from Year 2 (2018-2020) and Year 4 (2020-2022) follow-ups; 4,443 adolescents included in final analysis from ABCD cohort.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper analyzes ABCD Study data from Years 2 (2018-2020) and 4 (2020-2022) follow-ups; N=4,443 adolescents with complete screen time and cardiovascular outcome data.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Recreational screen time measured via Screen Time Questionnaire (self-reported hours on weekdays/weekends); categorized as low (0-4), medium (>4-8), or high (>8 hours/day). Physical activity measured via Fitbit step count.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Self-reported recreational screen time measured via Screen Time Questionnaire, categorized as low (0-4 h/day), medium (4-8 h/day), and high (>8 h/day).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multivariable linear regression examining associations between Year 2 screen time and step count with Year 4 cardiovascular disease risk factors (blood pressure, lipids, hemoglobin A1c). Multiple association tests reported with coefficients.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple multivariable linear and Poisson regression models testing screen time↔non-screen outcomes: systolic/diastolic blood pressure percentiles, hemoglobin A1c, total/HDL/non-HDL cholesterol. Example: high screen time (>8 h/day) vs. low (0-4 h/day) associated with diastolic BP increase (β=4.25, 95% CI 1.85-6.65, p=0.001).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric results include adjusted risk ratios and 95% CIs: high screen time (>8 h/day) ARR 1.48 [95% CI 1.42-2.56] for increased diastolic blood pressure; low step count ARR 2.35 [95% CI 1.88-2.94] for hypertensive-range BP; high screen time associated with 4.25-point increase in diastolic BP percentile.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric coefficients and risk ratios reported: β=4.25 (diastolic BP), RR=2.82 (non-HDL cholesterol >145 mg/dL), RR=2.15 (HDL cholesterol <40 mg/dL), RR=3.52 (step count low 1,000-6,000). All with 95% CIs and p-values.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis with two-year follow-up examining associations between Year 2 screen time/physical activity and Year 4 cardiovascular outcomes.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: multivariable linear and Poisson regression examining joint associations of screen time and step count (Year 2) with cardiovascular risk factors (Year 4), adjusted for sociodemographic and lifestyle covariates.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in BMC Public Health (2026).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article, BMC Public Health, 2026; doi:10.1186/s12889-026-26756-z.

title: ADHD and gaming addiction in adolescents: psychosocial mediators in the adolescent brain cognitive development study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Paper explicitly states analysis of ABCD Study data from Years 2-4 follow-up assessments; 11,868 aged 9-10 at baseline, with subset included in mediation analyses (n≈9,604 with complete mediator data).
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: PDF confirms analysis of U.S. ABCD Study baseline data (n=9,647 adolescents, mean age 13.1 years).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Symptoms of gaming addiction measured via Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ); ADHD measured via KSADS-COMP. Screen use operationalized as gaming-specific outcome/symptom.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Gaming addiction measured via Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (VGAQ), a qualifying screen/gaming variable that entered SEM analysis.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Longitudinal multilevel regression and parallel mediation models examining associations between ADHD diagnosis (Year 2) and VGAQ scores (Year 4), with nine psychosocial mediators tested (parental monitoring, family conflict, school involvement, peer involvement, parental warmth, school disengagement, school risk, prosocial behavior, neighborhood safety). Beta coefficients and indirect effects reported.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: SEM analysis reports direct pathway: ADHD → gaming addiction (β=0.39, p<0.001) and three indirect pathways through self-esteem, peer relationships, and academic stress (all p<0.001). Gaming addiction (screen variable) linked to non-screen variables (ADHD, self-esteem, peer relationships, academic stress).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric results include beta coefficients for ADHD → VGAQ association (β reported in text); mediation indirect effects with 95% CIs from 10,000 bootstrapped samples: prosocial peer involvement 7.4% of total effect, school involvement 5.8%, family conflict 5.1%; p-values reported for significance.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Direct effect: β=0.39, p<0.001; indirect effects: β=0.14 (self-esteem), β=0.08 (peer relationships), β=0.10 (academic stress), all p<0.001; direct effect after mediators: β=0.12, p<0.01; model fit indices reported (CFI=0.92, TLI=0.89, RMSEA=0.06).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original longitudinal mediation analysis examining associations between ADHD diagnosis and gaming addiction symptoms with psychosocial factors as mediators.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: cross-sectional SEM analysis of ABCD baseline data with mediation pathways.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Frontiers in Psychiatry (2026).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Frontiers in Psychiatry (2026).

FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data (N=7,614 participants, Year 2, ages 11-12 years) with longitudinal neuroimaging follow-up.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Social media use measured as screen variable; also examine cortical thickness and surface area as neural outcomes.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Social media use measured as hours per day on social media; social media addiction assessed via Social Media Addiction Questionnaire (SMAQ).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Inferential tests reported: linear mixed-effects regressions and multivariate analyses linking social media use to cortical morphology outcomes (thickness, surface area), adjusted for covariates.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple screen↔non-screen associations tested via FEMA mixed-effects algorithm. Table 2 reports associations between social media use/addiction and cortical morphology outcomes (thickness, surface area, volume) with t-tests and p-values.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Brain mapping results with beta coefficients, standard errors, t-values, and p-values reported in text and figures with numeric labels.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Extensive numeric results: Table 2 reports β coefficients with z-scores and p-values for ROI analyses (e.g., Superior frontal: β=2.3±1.2, z-score -1.916); Figure 1 visualizes z-scores; multiple ROIs with p-values reported.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD neuroimaging and behavioral data.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD neuroimaging data examining associations between social media use and brain structure.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article in NeuroImage.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in NeuroImage (2026).

title: Social media use, smoking expectancies, and nicotine experimentation in early adolescents: A prospective cohort study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: PDF confirms ABCD Study data: 'data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study' with baseline and follow-up assessments.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data (N=8,292 participants, Years 2-4, mean age 12.0 years at Year 2 baseline).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Social media use measured: 'self-reported social media use' at baseline and follow-up.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Social media time measured at Year 2 as hours and minutes per day (weekday/weekend weighted average).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Inferential tests: mixed-effects logistic regressions linking social media use to smoking expectancies and nicotine experimentation (non-screen outcomes).
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Longitudinal generalized structural equation modeling (GSEM) testing direct and indirect effects. Reports direct effect of social media on nicotine experimentation (B=0.138, 95% CI 0.081-0.196, p<.001) and indirect effect via smoking expectancies (B=0.009, 95% CI 0.003-0.014, p=.002).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Adjusted odds ratios, confidence intervals, and p-values reported.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Multiple numeric results in Table 2: β coefficients (B=0.138), 95% confidence intervals, p-values (<.001), indirect effect estimates (5.97% proportion mediated, 95% CI 1.27%-10.67%, p=.013).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Longitudinal prospective empirical analysis of ABCD cohort.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis with mediation pathway design examining ABCD data (Years 2-4 follow-up).
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article in The American Journal on Addictions.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in The American Journal on Addictions (2026).

title: Dating app use and depression symptoms in adolescents.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: PDF title and methods confirm 'Dating app use and depression symptoms in adolescents' using ABCD data.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper analyzes U.S. ABCD Study data (N=11,530 after multiple imputation, Year 5 cross-sectional data, ages 13-16 years).

FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Dating app use measured as lifetime use via single question: 'Have you ever used a dating app?' (yes/no/don't know). Dating apps qualify as digital screen/media variables.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Inferential test: association between dating app use (screen) and depression symptoms (non-screen mental health outcome) via statistical test.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multivariable linear regression analysis testing association between dating app use and depression symptoms. Adjusted model reports β coefficient with 95% CI and p-value (ß=0.42; 95% CI 0.08-0.76; p=0.017). Screen↔non-screen (mental health outcome).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric statistics reported (coefficients, p-values, confidence intervals).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric results reported: standardized β coefficient (0.42), 95% confidence interval (0.08-0.76), p-value (0.017). Sensitivity analyses report additional coefficients (ß=0.44; 95% CI 0.03-0.85; p=0.035 with expanded covariates).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Cross-sectional or longitudinal empirical analysis of ABCD data.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD Year 5 data examining dating app use and depression symptom associations.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article in BMC Research Notes.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in BMC Research Notes (2026).

title: The roles of delayed cortical maturation and lower anticipatory reward activation in predicting addictive screen use in youth.
journal: Biological psychiatry. Cognitive neuroscience and neuroimaging
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: PDF methods: 'The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study is an ongoing longitudinal study...We utilized data from ABCD Study Release 5.1 including baseline (Y0; ages 9-10) neuroimaging and two-year follow-up (Y2; ages 11-12)...' Analyzed ABCD sample with Y0 fMRI and Y2 addictive screen use data.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: ABCD Study data used explicitly. Baseline (Y0) ages 9-10 years, two-year follow-up (Y2) ages 11-12 years. N>3000 participants for resting-state fMRI, N=7396 and N=9166 for different analyses. Data from ABCD Release 5.1.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Multiple qualifying screen exposures measured: 'Addictive videogaming, addictive social media use, and addictive phone use questionnaires' and composite 'addictive screen use' (ASU) measure, all quantified as z-scored scales.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Addictive screen use measured at Y2 via three separate questionnaires (administered to subsample): addictive videogaming (N=4522), addictive social media use (N=3330), addictive phone use (N=4466). Summed scores created for each domain and cross-domain composite (max-ASU).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multiple inferential tests reported: mixed-effects regressions of cortical functional connectomic maturation (AFC) at Y0 predicting Y2 addictive videogaming, social media use, and phone use; indirect-effects mediation models; generalized linear models testing screen↔non-screen associations.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Mixed-effects regressions testing associations between neural predictors (Anchored Functional Connectomic maturation score, NAc anticipatory reward activation) and addictive screen use measures. Main associations: AFC at Y0 predicting max-ASU at Y2 (βM1=−0.056, p<0.001); AFC predicting addictive videogaming (βM1=−0.060, p<0.001); mediation analyses assessing cognitive task performance pathways to ASU.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Standardized regression coefficients (β), confidence intervals, p-values reported: e.g., β_M1 = -.056, CI [-.083, -.027], p < .001 for AFC-addictive screen use cross-domain; β_M2 = -.060, CI [-.087, -.031], p < .001 for AFC-addictive videogaming.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Multiple numeric coefficients reported: βM1=−0.056, CI [−0.083, −0.027], p<0.001 for max-ASU; βM1=−0.060, CI [−0.087, −0.031], p<0.001 for addictive videogaming; βM2=−0.035, p=0.072 for addictive phone use; negative binomial odds-ratio=0.97, CI [0.95, 0.99], Z=−4.07, p<0.001 for videogaming count.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Longitudinal neuroimaging study with original empirical analysis: mixed-effects regression, mediation analysis, moderation analysis, and cognitive task performance mediator analysis examining neural predictors of addictive screen use.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original longitudinal empirical analysis using ABCD neuroimaging data. Tests neurodevelopmental risk factors (cortical connectomic maturation, reward processing) predicting addictive screen use trajectories from Y0 to Y2.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Journal preproof article accepted for peer-reviewed publication in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging (2026).

title: Situating problematic gaming and psychotic-like experiences in the adolescent landscape of affordances: A cohort study.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: PDF methods: 'Data was drawn from the Version 5.1 of the Adolescent Brain and Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study...(Garavan et al., 2018), which includes visits between September 1st, 2016 and January 15, 2022.' Analyzed ABCD cohort at years 2 and 3 (ages 12-13).
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: ABCD Study data explicitly used. Participants from baseline recruited September 2016-January 2022 (N=11,868 ages 9-10). Present analyses focused on years 2 and 3 follow-up data (ages 12-13). N=6,467 youth in final sample after exclusions for missing data.
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Screen variable measured: 'problematic gaming was measured at years 2 and 3 with the Video Game Addiction Questionnaire...6 items based on addiction components' (qualifying problematic screen use scale).
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Problematic gaming measured at years 2 and 3 using Video Game Addiction Questionnaire (Bagot et al., 2022), adapted from Bergen Facebook Addiction Scale. Includes 6 items assessing addiction components: time thinking, need to play more, play to forget problems, withdrawal, relapse, and adverse consequences. Composite score with range 6-36.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Multiple inferential tests of screen↔non-screen: mixed-effects linear models examining associations between problematic gaming at Y2 and psychotic-like experiences (PLEs) at Y3; moderation and interaction analyses with environmental factors (peer, school, family environments); bivariate correlations and regression analyses.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Multiple mixed-effects linear models testing associations between problematic gaming (Y2) and psychotic-like experiences (Y3). Main findings reported with estimates and 95% CIs: school environment estimate=−0.15 [−0.21, −0.10], family environment estimate=−2.37 [−2.69, −2.05], peer environment estimate=0.50 [0.48, 0.52], problematic gaming Y2 to PLEs Y3 association with moderation effects tested.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Regression coefficients with 95% confidence intervals and p-values reported in tables/figures: e.g., 'Higher levels of problematic gaming at year 2 were significantly associated with higher levels of PLEs at year 3' with numeric estimates in Table 1 and Figure 2.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Extensive numeric results: associations reported as estimates with 95% confidence intervals throughout (e.g., −0.15 [−0.21, −0.10], −2.37 [−2.69, −2.05], 0.50 [0.48, 0.52], 0.13 [0.09, 0.17]); p-values derived from permutations and adjusted for multiple comparisons; correlation matrix with Pearson's r values provided.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Longitudinal prospective cohort study with original empirical analysis: linear mixed models examining associations between Y2 problematic gaming and Y3 PLEs with covariates.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD cohort data. Longitudinal design examining associations between Y2 problematic gaming and Y3 PLEs, with moderation by school/family/peer environment variables. Linear mixed models account for clustering (sites, families).
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Journal of Behavioral Addictions, 2026 (published online January 26, 2026).

FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical analysis: longitudinal predictive model using baseline neuroimaging to predict 4-year outcome. CPM is a machine-learning mediation/moderation approach.

FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in NeuroImage, 2026, DOI: 10.1016/j.neuroimage.2026.121829.

title: Connectome-based predictive modelling of problematic gaming in youth from the ABCD study.

title: The association between screen time and cognitive function in children: The partially mediating role of putamen volume.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Methods section states analysis of ABCD study participants with longitudinal neuroimaging data.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly states: 'Using data from the Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development study of nearly 10,000 children aged 9–10 years' and uses ABCD Study's 5.0 release from the National Institute of Mental Health Data Archive.

FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis using linear mixed-effects models with mediation analysis of ABCD data examining screen time associations with cognitive function and putamen volume.

FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using Gaussian Graphical Models and Graphical Vector Autoregressive Models (GVAR) examining bidirectional temporal network associations between screen activities and irritability across three timepoints in ABCD data.

FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Paper analyzes data from U.S. ABCD Study (release 5.1, June 2016 to October 15, 2018), with 3419 participants across 3 waves (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14 years).
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Data from ABCD Study, release 5.1, collected June 1, 2016 to October 15, 2018. Study followed 3419 youth across waves 1-3 (ages 9-11, 12-13, 13-14 years).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Paper measures screen use via Youth Screen Time Survey (total daily screen time calculated as weighted mean across weekdays/weekends), explicitly included as mediator variable in models examining parental factors and adolescent sleep.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Screen use measured by youth-reported Youth Screen Time Survey at wave 2 (baseline screens). Total daily screen use calculated as weighted mean across weekdays and weekends in hours per day (mean 2.60, SD 2.17).
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Inferential statistical tests linking screen use to non-screen sleep outcomes reported throughout: linear mixed models with screen use predicting sleep duration, timing, chronotype, quality, and regularity; mediation analyses showing indirect associations of parental factors through screen use to sleep outcomes.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Screen use↔non-screen (sleep) associations reported across multiple sleep outcomes. Direct associations: higher parental monitoring associated with better sleep quality (β=0.04, p=.04); this association mediated through decreased screen use (indirect β=-0.004, 95% CI -0.008 to -0.002, proportion mediated 73%). Screen use partially mediates parental psychopathology→sleep quality association (indirect β=-0.05, 95% CI -0.06 to -0.04).
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Numeric coefficients reported: standardized β values (e.g., parental monitoring→sleep quality via screen use β=0.004, 95% CI 0.002-0.008; family conflict→sleep quality via screen use β=-0.003, 95% CI -0.006 to -0.001); 95% CIs consistently reported.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Numeric results reported in Figure 1 forest plot and Figure 2 mediation models. Direct effects of parental factors on sleep: β=0.04 (95% CI 0.01-0.08) for monitoring→sleep quality; β=0.06 (95% CI 0.02-0.11) for psychopathology→sleep timing. Screen use indirect paths: β=-0.004 (95% CI -0.002 to -0.008); β=-0.05 (95% CI -0.06 to -0.04).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Preregistered prospective longitudinal cohort study (registered on Open Science Framework) examining associations between parental factors and sleep outcomes 4 years later, with screen use and emotional regulation as mediators.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis. Linear mixed models examining associations between wave-1 parental factors and wave-3 sleep outcomes (duration, timing, chronotype, quality, regularity) with wave-2 mediators (screen use, emotional regulation). Mediation paths tested via path analysis with 1000 simulations.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in JAMA Network Open (September 11, 2025), clearly a full-text peer-reviewed publication.
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in JAMA Network Open (2025; doi:10.1001/jamanetworkopen.2025.31333).

FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Screen time measured via ABCD Screen Time Questionnaire (STQ) at baseline and year 4 for video games and social media. Problematic screen use (PSU) measured via VGAQ, SMAQ, MPIQ for video games, social media, and smartphones respectively.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Reports linear mixed effects models testing baseline individual differences (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognition) as predictors of later screen time. FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Linear mixed-effects models tested baseline individual differences (impulsivity, motivation, psychopathology, cognition) as predictors of later screen time and PSU. FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Multiple β coefficients, standard errors, and p-values reported in Table 3 main effects. Examples: urgency→video game ST (β=+.07, SE=.01, p<.001); reward sensitivity→video game ST (β=+.04, SE=.01, p<.001); general cognitive ability→video game ST (β=−.10, SE=.08, p<.001).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis: prospective longitudinal design using linear mixed effects models examining baseline predictors of year 4 screen time outcomes with family-level random effects and site-level adjustments.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Longitudinal prospective analysis of ABCD data examining baseline individual differences predicting later screen use at year 4. Original empirical analysis.

The paper identifies and validates seven latent neurodevelopmental profiles based on fMRI activation patterns and cognitive/behavioral measures. This is a measurement/psychometric paper without external validity analyses linking screen to non-screen variables.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Cannot evaluate. No screen variable measured, so screen↔non-screen associations cannot exist.
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: No screen↔non-screen associations tested. Paper identifies and validates seven latent neurodevelopmental profiles based on fMRI activation and cognitive/behavioral measures, but screen time is not analyzed in relation to profiles or outcomes.

title: Passive Sensing of Preteens' Smartphone Use: An Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Cohort Substudy.
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Title: 'Passive Sensing of Preteens' Smartphone Use: An Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Cohort Substudy.' PDF introduction: 'The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study is a landmark longitudinal study of nearly 12,000 children aged 9-10 years.' Pilot substudy used ABCD sites with N=67 participants.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly states 'The Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) study used passive, objective phone app methods for assessing smartphone use in a pilot sample of children and to assess the feasibility of passive sensing in a larger longitudinal sample.' Study population: N=67 participants aged 9-12 years from ABCD substudy (EARS substudy).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Title and PDF explicitly measure 'passive sensing of smartphone use' via EARS (Effortless Assessment of Risk States) app. PDF methods describe passive objective measurement of app use by time and type (streaming, YouTube, communication, gaming, social media, SMS, reading, music, news). Separate measure of Screen Time Questionnaire (self-report) also collected for comparison.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Paper measures multiple screen variables via passive sensing: total smartphone screen time, app categories (streaming, YouTube, communication, gaming, social media, SMS, reading, music, news), and time-of-day patterns of use. Passive sensing via EARS app measured actual usage minutes per day across app types.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: PDF examines relationship between passive smartphone screen use (via EARS app) and self-report/parent-report of screen time (non-screen? contextual measure). More directly: PDF examines acceptability/feasibility outcomes and correlations between objective and subjective screen use measures. PDF Figure 1 shows Pearson correlation between EARS passive sensing and self-report (r=.49, P<.001) and parent-report (r=.1, P=.43). These represent screen↔screen relationships. Additionally, PDF reports demographic differences in app use by sex, with girls showing significantly higher reading app use (Figure 2: F(1,65)=5.55, P=.02).
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Paper reports no inferential statistical tests linking screen variables to non-screen outcomes. Results are purely descriptive: correlations between self-report and passive sensing (r=.49, p<.001; r=.1, p=.43 for overall use), app category breakdowns, and agreement analysis between measurement methods. No screen↔non-screen associations examined.
FT_IC3_RESOLVE_evidence: Figure 2 reports F-statistic for sex differences in app use: girls showed significantly higher reading app use (F(1,65)=5.55, p=.02). Per E14, sociodemographic (sex) as predictor of screen use constitutes screen↔non-screen association. Per E5, measurement papers that report external outcome associations meet FT-IC3.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: PDF reports numeric results: Figure 1 shows correlations (r=.49, P<.001 for EARS vs self-report; r=.1, P=.43 for EARS vs parent-report). Table 1 lists app use times with means and SDs (e.g., Streaming 1h 57min, YouTube 1h 18min). Figure 2 reports F-statistic and p-value for sex differences in reading app use (F(1,65)=5.55, P=.02). Table 2 reports means and SDs for daily device use pre/post sensing.
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: No numeric results for screen↔non-screen associations reported because FT_IC3 is not met. Paper provides descriptive statistics (means, SDs, correlation coefficients) but no inferential test statistics linking screen to behavioral/health outcomes.
FT_IC4_RESOLVE_evidence: Figure 2 reports F-statistic (F(1,65)=5.55) and p-value (p=.02) for sex differences in reading app use. Additionally, Figure 1 reports Pearson correlations (r=.49, p<.001 for EARS vs self-report; r=.1, p=.43 for EARS vs parent-report).
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical analysis. PDF describes a pilot substudy implementing passive monitoring via EARS app in ABCD sample, with descriptive statistics of app use, correlation analysis comparing passive/objective measurement to self- and parent-report, and demographic analysis of app use differences by sex.
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical study: measurement/validation study of ABCD's new passive sensing protocol for smartphone use. Although primarily psychometric in nature, paper meets criteria because it analyzes ABCD data using novel measurement methods in a real cohort.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in JMIR Mental Health (2021).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in JMIR Mental Health 2021, not a preprint or abstract duplicate.

title: The association between adverse childhood experiences (ACEs), bullying victimization, and internalizing and externalizing problems among early adolescents â¦

FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Beta coefficients with 95% confidence intervals reported in Table 2. Examples: ACEs (B=1.02, 95% CI 1.27-2.58); Bullying victimization (B=1.28, 95% CI 0.72-1.84).

title: Digital Media, Genetics, and Risk for ADHD Symptoms in Children: A Longitudinal Study
FT_IC1_C1_evidence: Nivins et al. (2026) study analyzes data from 8,324 children in the U.S. ABCD Study followed annually for 4 years. Methods explicitly describe longitudinal ABCD cohort enrollment (ages 9-10) at baseline 2016-2018.
FT_IC1_C2_evidence: Paper explicitly analyzes data from 8,324 children from the U.S. Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with baseline enrollment at ages 9-10 years (2016-2018) and 4-year longitudinal follow-up (T0-T4).
FT_IC2_C1_evidence: Digital media (DM) use measured including social media use, playing video games, and watching television/videos. Youth Screen Time Survey captured hours/day across multiple modalities.
FT_IC2_C2_evidence: Paper measures digital media (DM) use via Youth Screen Time Survey across multiple types: social media, playing video games, and watching television/videos, all measured as hours/day at baseline and follow-up visits and analyzed as exposures.
FT_IC3_C1_evidence: Linear mixed-effects models examine longitudinal associations between digital media use and ADHD-related symptoms. Separate DM use × time interactions tested for social media, gaming, and TV/videos.
FT_IC3_C2_evidence: Longitudinal linear mixed models test associations between average DM use and ADHD-related symptoms (measured via parent-reported Child Behavior Checklist). Main finding: social media use × time interaction β=0.03 (SE=0.01), P<.001 for inattention symptoms; cross-lagged panel models confirm directionality.
FT_IC4_C1_evidence: Multiple numeric coefficients reported. Examples: Average social media use×time (β [SE], 0.03 [0.01]; P<.001); gaming×time (β [SE], −0.05 [0.01]; P<.001); TV/videos×time (β [SE], −0.05 [0.01]; P<.001).
FT_IC4_C2_evidence: Multiple numeric coefficients reported: β=0.03 (SE=0.01) for social media×time on inattention; cumulative 4-year effect β=0.15 (SE=0.03); cross-lagged path coefficients β=0.03 (SE=0.01), P=.004; all p-values and CIs provided in Table 2.
FT_IC5_C1_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD data examining associations between digital media use and ADHD-related symptoms over 4-year follow-up, accounting for genetic predisposition (PGS-ADHD).
FT_IC5_C2_evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis of ABCD data using linear mixed-effects models with time-varying exposures and cross-lagged panel models to examine longitudinal associations between DM use and ADHD symptoms over 4 years.
FT_IC6_C1_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Pediatrics Open Science (2026).
FT_IC6_C2_evidence: Peer-reviewed journal article published in Pediatrics Open Science (Volume 2, Issue 1, 2026), original research article with complete empirical analyses.
